column3: 如何区分和治疗剂峰异动症与双相异动症？
column4: How to differentiate and treat peak-dose versus biphasic dyskinesia?
column5:
Peak-dose dyskinesia occurs at maximal levodopa effect and is usually treated by reducing or smoothing dopaminergic peaks, whereas biphasic dyskinesia occurs as levodopa levels rise and fall and is often improved by increasing or making dopaminergic stimulation more continuous.
Clinical differentiation
Peak‑dose dyskinesia (PDD)
 Timing: Mid‑dose, when levodopa effect is strongest (clear ON) (Hutny et al., 2021; Sica et al., 2021).
 Pattern: Choreiform, often generalized; patient otherwise mobile and “too ON.”
 Relation to dose: Worse with higher single levodopa doses; often absent at low doses.
 
Biphasic dyskinesia (BID)
 Timing: At beginning and end of each levodopa dose (rising and falling plasma levels), often with comparatively calmer movement at true peak (Hutny et al., 2021; Tsunemi et al., 2021).
 Pattern: More dystonic or ballistic, frequently in legs; can be very disabling.
 Relation to dose: Can improve when dopaminergic stimulation is increased or made continuous (Hutny et al., 2021; Tsunemi et al., 2021).
 
Treatment principles
Peak-dose dyskinesia
 Use lowest effective levodopa dose and counsel that higher doses increase dyskinesia risk (Pringsheim et al., 2021; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Strategies: 
 Slightly reduce individual levodopa doses, possibly increase dosing frequency to preserve ON time (Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Add amantadine, the only drug with consistent evidence for dyskinesia reduction (Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Consider switching to extended‑release levodopa or adding COMT/MAO‑B inhibitors to smooth peaks, while monitoring for worsening dyskinesia (De Bie et al., 2025; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 For refractory, disabling PDD, GPi or STN deep brain stimulation is evidence‑based to reduce dyskinesia and motor fluctuations (Hutny et al., 2021; De Bie et al., 2025).
 
 
 
Biphasic dyskinesia
 Pathophysiology: Occurs when levodopa level crosses a “dyskinesia threshold” during both rise and fall; may improve if patient spends more time in a stable ON range (Hutny et al., 2021).
 Oral adjustments: 
 Increase dopaminergic load (higher or more frequent levodopa, add dopamine agonist) to reduce time in low/intermediate range (Hutny et al., 2021; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Avoid excessive dose fractionation that lengthens time in the biphasic window.
 
 
 Continuous therapies: 
 Intrajejunal levodopa infusion (LCIG) can reduce OFF and provide ON without troublesome dyskinesia; disabling diphasic dyskinesia after LCIG may respond to higher morning bolus and higher continuous flow, often with bedtime ER levodopa or agonists (Tsunemi et al., 2021).
 Other continuous options: subcutaneous levodopa or apomorphine, and DBS, to reduce fluctuations and biphasic phenomena (Tsunemi et al., 2021; De Bie et al., 2025; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 
 
 
Conclusion
Peak‑dose dyskinesia worsens with levodopa peaks and is managed mainly by dose reduction and antidyskinetic therapy (amantadine, DBS), whereas biphasic dyskinesia is linked to transitions into and out of ON and often requires more continuous or higher dopaminergic stimulation (tighter dosing, agonists, or device‑aided therapies such as LCIG or DBS).

column3: 帕金森治疗中“持续多巴胺能刺激（CDS）”的概念是什么？
column4: What is the concept of continuous dopaminergic stimulation in PD treatment?
column5:
Continuous dopaminergic stimulation (CDS) aims to provide a steady, non‑pulsatile dopamine signal to reduce motor fluctuations and dyskinesia in Parkinson’s disease.
Concept and Rationale
 Standard oral levodopa produces peaks and troughs in brain dopamine, leading over time to motor fluctuations (ON–OFF) and dyskinesias as nigrostriatal neurons are lost and can no longer buffer intermittent doses (Höllerhage et al., 2024; Sica et al., 2021).
 CDS proposes that smoother, more constant receptor stimulation better mimics physiological dopamine tone and may limit these complications. Motor fluctuations arise when “the number of dopaminergic neurons… becomes insufficient to release levodopa continuously,” so patients cycle between ON and OFF within each dosing interval (Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Advanced therapies were developed specifically to approximate CDS when oral regimens no longer give stable control (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025; Sica et al., 2021).
 
How CDS Is Implemented
1. Pharmacologic strategies
 Extended‑release levodopa and once‑daily dopamine agonists (pramipexole ER, ropinirole ER, rotigotine) lengthen plasma half‑life and reduce pulsatile swings; these are rated efficacious for motor fluctuations (De Bie et al., 2025; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 Adjunct COMT or MAO‑B inhibitors (entacapone, opicapone, rasagiline, safinamide) prolong levodopa or dopamine action and reduce OFF time, moving patients closer to CDS (De Bie et al., 2025; Höllerhage et al., 2024).
 
2. Device‑aided continuous infusions
 Continuous intestinal levodopa infusion and continuous subcutaneous levodopa or apomorphine provide near‑constant dopaminergic input and are considered (likely) efficacious for treating motor fluctuations in levodopa‑treated patients (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025; Fernandes et al., 2025).
 These “pump therapies” are recommended when optimized oral regimens fail to control severe fluctuations, as part of device‑assisted therapy algorithms (Reese et al., 2025; Höllerhage et al., 2024; Sica et al., 2021).
 
Conclusion
CDS is the guiding principle behind many advanced PD treatments: replace intermittent, high‑amplitude dosing with steadier dopaminergic delivery to minimize motor fluctuations and dyskinesia while maintaining symptom control.

column3: 治疗帕金森病合并抑郁症的安全有效药物有哪些？
column4: What are the safe and effective antidepressants for Parkinson's disease?
column5:
For depression in Parkinson’s disease, evidence supports using standard antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs, some TCAs, MAO‑Is, bupropion), but choice must prioritize tolerability, motor effects, cognition, orthostatic BP, and falls rather than PD-specific efficacy.
Figure 1: Consensus on using standard antidepressants in PD
Evidence on Options in PD
Direct PD‑specific antidepressant trials are limited, so most guidance extrapolates from general depression plus PD safety data (Chmiel et al., 2024; , 2021).
Generally used in PD (with supportive safety/efficacy signals):
 SSRIs (e.g., citalopram, sertraline, paroxetine): Widely prescribed in PD and considered effective and reasonably safe, though can cause hyponatremia, orthostatic hypotension, and falls in older adults (Chmiel et al., 2024; Van Poelgeest et al., 2021; , 2021).
 SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine): Frequently used; duloxetine/venlafaxine also target pain, which is common in PD, and have evidence for analgesia in chronic pain conditions (Sánchez-Salcedo et al., 2021; Ferreira et al., 2023; Chmiel et al., 2024).
 Bupropion: Used because it enhances dopaminergic and noradrenergic transmission and may theoretically support PD motor circuits (Chmiel et al., 2024).
 MAO inhibitors: Non‑selective MAO‑Is have demonstrated efficacy and safety for depression, but in PD are less favored because of interaction risks with MAO‑B inhibitors used for motor symptoms (Chmiel et al., 2024; , 2021).
 
Caution / second‑line in PD:
 Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs): May be more effective for pain (Sánchez-Salcedo et al., 2021; Ferreira et al., 2023), but in PD carry higher risk of cognitive worsening, psychosis, anticholinergic side effects, arrhythmia, and falls (Sánchez-Salcedo et al., 2021; Van Poelgeest et al., 2021). Often reserved for selected, closely monitored patients.
 
Practical Selection Considerations

 (Sánchez-Salcedo et al., 2021; Ferreira et al., 2023; Chmiel et al., 2024)

 (Sánchez-Salcedo et al., 2021; Van Poelgeest et al., 2021; Reilly et al., 2023; Chmiel et al., 2024)
 
  
Figure 2: Choosing antidepressants by PD comorbidities
Conclusion
In Parkinson’s disease, SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram, paroxetine), SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) and bupropion are generally regarded as the safest first‑line antidepressants, chosen and dosed with attention to falls, orthostatic hypotension, cognition, and drug–drug interactions. TCAs can be effective but are usually second‑line due to side‑effect burden in this population.

column3: 如何处理帕金森病患者出现的视幻觉和精神病性症状？
column4: How to manage visual hallucinations and psychosis in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Management of visual hallucinations and psychosis in Parkinson’s disease focuses first on addressing triggers and medications, then using PD‑sparing antipsychotics when needed.
Figure 1: Consensus on PD-sparing antipsychotics for psychosis
1. Assess and Address Triggers
 Psychosis affects ~30–50% of people with PD over the disease course (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 Screen regularly for early features (vivid dreams, misidentifications, minor hallucinations, sensed presence) and involve family, as patients may not report them spontaneously (Witt et al., 2024; Toh et al., 2023).
 General preventive/first‑line measures: maintain circadian rhythm, optimize lighting and sensory aids, keep a familiar environment, treat infections/dehydration, and avoid unnecessary drugs with anticholinergic, sedative, or antiglutamatergic effects (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025).
 
2. Adjust Parkinson Medications
 Guidelines recommend, where possible, reducing or simplifying dopaminergic therapy to the lowest dose that controls motor symptoms while alleviating psychosis (Witt et al., 2024; Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 Review all psychosis‑provoking drugs (e.g., dopamine agonists) and polypharmacy; taper the most delirogenic/psychotogenic agents first (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2022).
 
3. Antipsychotic Treatment
 If symptoms are distressing or dangerous despite optimization: 
 Clozapine and quetiapine are preferred because they have lower risk of worsening motor symptoms compared with other antipsychotics (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 Use shortest duration and lowest effective dose, with careful monitoring (e.g., blood counts for clozapine) (Witt et al., 2024; Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 Pimavanserin (5‑HT2A inverse agonist) has accumulating evidence and is considered a treatment option where available, though early data suggest possible higher early hospitalisation and mortality, so monitoring is essential (Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 
 
 Typical antipsychotics and most other second‑generation agents are generally avoided due to motor worsening and other adverse effects (Witt et al., 2024; Chow et al., 2023).
 
Non‑dopaminergic adjuncts
 Cholinesterase inhibitor rivastigmine may reduce falls and has theoretical and limited clinical support for improving hallucinations, but evidence for psychosis control is still uncertain (Reilly et al., 2023).
 5‑HT3 antagonists (e.g., ondansetron) are under investigation for hallucinations based on sensory gating mechanisms but remain experimental (Tsitsipa et al., 2022).
 
Summary Table
   Step
 Key action in PD psychosis
 Citations
 
    1
 Screen, correct medical/environmental triggers
 (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Toh et al., 2023)
 
  2
 Rationalize/reduce dopaminergic & delirogenic drugs
 (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Van Wamelen et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022)
 
  3
 Add clozapine or quetiapine; consider pimavanserin
 (Witt et al., 2024; Petersen et al., 2025; Van Wamelen et al., 2022)
 
  4
 Consider rivastigmine/experimental approaches
 (Reilly et al., 2023; Tsitsipa et al., 2022; Van Wamelen et al., 2022)
 
  
Figure 2: Stepwise management strategy for PD psychosis
Conclusion
Management of PD visual hallucinations and psychosis is stepwise: optimize environment and comorbidities, carefully reduce offending medications, then add PD‑sparing antipsychotics (clozapine, quetiapine, or pimavanserin) with close safety monitoring. Regular screening and involving caregivers are crucial.

column3: 匹马吉林（Pimavanserin）对比喹硫平治疗幻觉的疗效如何？
column4: What is the efficacy of pimavanserin versus quetiapine for PD psychosis?
column5:
Evidence suggests pimavanserin provides stronger antipsychotic efficacy with less motor/cognitive impact than quetiapine for Parkinson’s disease (PD) psychosis.
Figure 1: Consensus on pimavanserin vs quetiapine in PD
Comparative efficacy and safety
A large, high‑quality network meta‑analysis of 19 trials (1242 patients) with PD psychosis compared pimavanserin, quetiapine, clozapine and other antipsychotics (Witt et al., 2024). Key findings:
 Efficacy on psychosis: Only clozapine and pimavanserin showed clear suppression of psychotic symptoms without worsening motor function (Witt et al., 2024).
 Quetiapine: Did not show robust antipsychotic benefit in the network meta‑analysis; instead it worsened cognition (Witt et al., 2024).
 Motor symptoms: Pimavanserin improved psychosis without motor worsening, whereas many other agents, including some used in PD, worsened parkinsonism (Witt et al., 2024).
 Guideline interpretation: The German PD guideline therefore notes that pimavanserin is effective for PD psychosis with minimal motor effects and highlights it as a promising way to resolve the “efficacy vs. safety” dilemma seen with clozapine and quetiapine (Witt et al., 2024).
 
Quetiapine remains in the guideline only as an option when cognition is preserved, with explicit warning about its low evidence for efficacy (Witt et al., 2024).
Summary comparison

 Pimavanserin
 Quetiapine
 Citations
 
    Psychosis reduction
 Clear benefit
 Weak/uncertain benefit
 (Witt et al., 2024)
 
  Motor worsening
 None detected
 Acceptable but less well studied
 (Witt et al., 2024)
 
  Cognition
 Neutral in trials

 (Witt et al., 2024)
 
  Evidence base
 RCTs + network meta‑analysis
 Small, low‑quality studies
 (Witt et al., 2024)
 
  
Figure 2: Head-to-head evidence favoring pimavanserin
Conclusion
Available comparative evidence (indirect, via network meta‑analysis) indicates pimavanserin is more clearly effective and cognitively/motor‑sparing than quetiapine for PD psychosis, though cost and availability limit its use in some regions. Direct head‑to‑head RCTs are still lacking.

column3: 帕金森病痴呆（PDD）的推荐治疗方案是什么？
column4: What are the recommended treatments for Parkinson's disease dementia?
column5:
For Parkinson’s disease dementia (PDD), the main evidence‑based treatments are cholinesterase inhibitors (especially rivastigmine), with memantine and structured non‑drug interventions as secondary options.
Pharmacological treatments
1. Cholinesterase inhibitors (first‑line)
 A 2025 systematic review of guidelines and systematic reviews found strong, consistent recommendations for cholinesterase inhibitors in PDD, with the best evidence for rivastigmine (Pourzinal et al., 2025).
 Rivastigmine improves cognition and global function in mild–moderate PDD and can also reduce psychotic symptoms in PDD (Reilly et al., 2023; Moyaert et al., 2023).
 Other cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, galantamine) are also endorsed, but with less PDD‑specific data than rivastigmine (Pourzinal et al., 2025; Moyaert et al., 2023).
 
2. Memantine (second‑line / adjunct)
 The same guideline review found mixed or limited support for memantine in PDD; effects on cognition and function are weaker and less consistent than cholinesterase inhibitors (Pourzinal et al., 2025).
 In broader dementia literature, memantine has only weak level‑4 evidence for quality‑of‑life benefits and is not recommended as primary therapy (Luxton et al., 2025).
 
3. General medication strategy in PD with dementia
 Discontinue or minimize drugs that impair cognition: anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, amantadine, selegiline, high‑potency D2 antipsychotics, tricyclics, bladder anticholinergics (Pourzinal et al., 2025).
 When antipsychotics are needed for psychosis, use PD‑sparing agents at the lowest dose, shortest duration, because they can worsen motor function and cognition (Pourzinal et al., 2025).
 
Non‑pharmacological and care interventions
 Exercise, cognitive rehabilitation, dual‑task training, dance, yoga and other structured activities improve cognition or reduce risk of impairment in PD and are strongly recommended as part of management (Pourzinal et al., 2025).
 In general dementia, group cognitive stimulation therapy, reminiscence therapy, occupational therapy, music and exercise have level 1–2 evidence for improving quality of life and should be combined with drugs when possible (Luxton et al., 2025).
 
Conclusion
Management of PDD centers on rivastigmine (or another cholinesterase inhibitor) plus optimization of PD medications and structured non‑pharmacological therapies, with memantine considered when first‑line options are insufficient or not tolerated.

column3: 如何管理帕金森病引起的神经源性直立性低血压（体位性头晕）？
column4: How to manage neurogenic orthostatic hypotension in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Management of neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (nOH) in Parkinson’s disease combines non‑pharmacologic measures with pressor drugs such as droxidopa, midodrine, and fludrocortisone, with pyridostigmine as an adjunct.
Overall approach
Management aims to reduce symptoms and falls, not to normalize blood pressure (Wahba et al., 2021). First steps are to confirm nOH (orthostatic BP/HR testing) and review reversible causes and medications that worsen hypotension (e.g., excessive dopaminergic dose, antihypertensives, diuretics) (Katsi et al., 2021; Wahba et al., 2021). nOH often coexists with supine hypertension, so both must be monitored (Katsi et al., 2021; Wahba et al., 2021).
Non‑pharmacologic strategies
   Strategy
 Effect/notes
 Citations
 
    Increased fluids and salt (if no HF/CKD)
 Expand plasma volume
 (Katsi et al., 2021; Wahba et al., 2021)
 
  Head‑up sleeping position
 Reduces nocturnal diuresis and daytime nOH
 (Wahba et al., 2021)
 
  Slow position changes, small frequent meals
 Reduce postural and post‑prandial drops
 (Katsi et al., 2021; Wahba et al., 2021)
 
  Compression garments/abdominal binders
 Reduce venous pooling, improve venous return
 (Wahba et al., 2021)
 
  Resistance/leg exercises
 Reduced BP fall during orthostatic stress in PD
 (Van Wamelen et al., 2022)
 
  
Figure 1: Non-drug strategies to reduce nOH symptoms
Pharmacologic treatment in PD‑related nOH
 Droxidopa (NE precursor) – In PD, randomized trials show improved dizziness/light‑headedness and other nOH symptoms and higher upright systolic BP vs placebo, now supported by level 1A evidence for OH in PD (Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 Midodrine (α‑agonist) – Widely used norepinephrine “replacer”; particularly useful when sympathetic tone is low, but increases risk of supine hypertension (Wahba et al., 2021).
 Fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid) – Small RCT in PD showed 37% improvement in diastolic BP drop on standing (level 2B evidence) (Van Wamelen et al., 2022). Monitor for edema, hypokalemia, supine hypertension.
 Pyridostigmine – Systematic review of nOH (various etiologies including PD) found modest improvements in orthostatic BP, minimal risk of supine hypertension, mainly cholinergic side effects; best used as adjunct in refractory cases (Holder et al., 2024).
 Treatment selection – Expert reviews recommend choosing between “NE enhancers” (pyridostigmine) vs “replacers” (midodrine, droxidopa) based on residual sympathetic tone and BP profile (Wahba et al., 2021).
 
Deep brain stimulation may give short‑term improvement of cardiovascular symptoms including OH, mainly via medication reduction, but direct effects on orthostatic BP are inconsistent and evidence remains limited (Cucinotta et al., 2024).

Conclusion
Management of nOH in Parkinson’s disease should start with education, volume/behavioral measures, and compression, followed by droxidopa or midodrine and/or fludrocortisone, with pyridostigmine as an adjunct in refractory cases, while actively monitoring and managing coexisting supine hypertension.

column3: 帕金森患者严重便秘的循证治疗方法有哪些？
column4: What are the evidence-based treatments for severe constipation in PD?
column5:
For severe constipation in Parkinson’s disease, the best evidence supports standard constipation drugs (especially polyethylene glycol and stimulant laxatives) plus pro/synbiotics specifically studied in PD.
Evidence in Parkinson’s disease
Probiotics / synbiotics (PD‑specific RCTs)
 Multiple RCTs in PD show that probiotic or synbiotic supplements significantly improve constipation symptoms and stool frequency, and this effect is consistent across trials (Prasad et al., 2025; Leta et al., 2021).
 A 2025 review of RCTs in PD concluded that pro‑ and synbiotics “consistently demonstrated efficacy in improving constipation symptoms”, though effects on motor symptoms were mixed (Prasad et al., 2025).
 A systematic review focused on probiotics in PD found clinical studies reporting reduced constipation and overall non‑motor symptom burden, alongside anti‑inflammatory changes (Leta et al., 2021).
 
Practical role
 Typically used in addition to standard laxatives in moderate–severe constipation.
 Optimal strains, doses, and treatment duration are still uncertain, and more robust trials are needed (Prasad et al., 2025; Leta et al., 2021).
 
General evidence‑based constipation treatments (applied to PD)
While not PD‑specific, strong evidence from chronic idiopathic constipation informs practice for severe PD constipation:
First‑line pharmacologic options
   Class / agent
 Evidence summary
 Citations
 
    Polyethylene glycol (PEG)
 High‑quality RCTs; first‑line osmotic laxative with strong guideline recommendation and long‑term safety
 (Chang et al., 2023; Rao & Brenner, 2021)
 
  Senna
 Good RCT data; grade A recommendation as stimulant laxative
 (Rao & Brenner, 2021)
 
  Other OTC options
 Moderate evidence for psyllium, magnesium salts, bisacodyl, sodium picosulfate, fruit laxatives (e.g., prunes)
 (Rao & Brenner, 2021)
 
  Prescription agents
 Linaclotide, plecanatide, prucalopride strongly recommended for chronic constipation in adults when OTC fails
 (Chang et al., 2023)
 
  
Figure 1: Key evidence-based laxatives for severe constipation
These agents are commonly used in PD (with attention to hydration and fall risk) when lifestyle measures and basic laxatives are insufficient.
Conclusion
Management of severe constipation in PD should combine standard evidence‑based laxatives (PEG ± stimulant, then secretagogues/prokinetics if needed) with PD‑specific evidence‑supported probiotics/synbiotics, tailored to tolerability, comorbidities, and severity, recognizing that optimal probiotic regimens still require further study.

column3: 如何治疗帕金森病中的睡眠碎片化和失眠？
column4: How to treat sleep fragmentation and insomnia in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Sleep fragmentation and insomnia in Parkinson’s disease are managed by treating reversible causes, optimizing dopaminergic therapy, and using targeted behavioral, light-based, and selected pharmacologic treatments.
1. Identify causes and optimize PD therapy
Sleep in PD is usually disrupted by multiple factors (nocturnal “off” motor symptoms, nocturia, pain, RLS, RBD, depression, circadian dysfunction), so these should be systematically screened and treated first (Van Wamelen et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 Guidelines recommend adjusting night-time dopaminergic therapy when nocturnal wearing-off or akinesia fragment sleep, using long‑acting dopamine agonists, slow‑release levodopa, or pump therapy, then re‑evaluating sleep (Van Wamelen et al., 2022; Milane et al., 2023; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
Key pharmacologic options for insomnia/sleep fragmentation in PD
   Treatment
 Evidence in PD
 Citations
 
    Rotigotine patch
 Level 1A evidence; improves PD sleep scales and non‑motor burden without worsening daytime sleepiness (Van Wamelen et al., 2022).
 (Van Wamelen et al., 2022)
 
  Night levodopa/carbidopa/entacapone
 Improved sleep onset, maintenance and PDSS scores in PD with worse baseline sleep (Milane et al., 2023).
 (Milane et al., 2023)
 
  Melatonin (2–3 mg SR)
 RCTs and systematic review: safe, modestly improves subjective sleep quality; limited effect on daytime sleepiness/RBD (Yalçin et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 (Yalçin et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025)
 
  Hypnotics/antidepressants (eszopiclone, zolpidem, low‑dose doxepin, trazodone)
 German guideline lists as options after excluding other disorders, following general insomnia guidance (Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 (Fanciulli et al., 2025)
 
  
Figure 1: Main drug options specifically studied in PD insomnia
2. Non‑pharmacologic therapies
 Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT‑I): Two RCTs and several uncontrolled trials show CBT‑I (individual, group, or telehealth) reduces insomnia severity, wake time, and night awakenings, with gains in mood and quality of life (Lopes et al., 2021).
 Bright light therapy (BLT): Multiple studies in PD report improved sleep quality, insomnia, sleep fragmentation, daytime sleepiness, mood and motor symptoms (Yalçin et al., 2025; Lopes et al., 2021).
 Exercise and physical training: Intensive physical training improves sleep quality and efficiency versus sleep‑hygiene alone in PD (Fanciulli et al., 2025); less sleep is associated with more sedentary behavior (Khan et al., 2024).
 General measures: Sleep‑hygiene counseling, regular schedule, limiting daytime naps, treating comorbid sleep apnea/RLS/RBD and pain are guideline‑recommended (Van Wamelen et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2022; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 
Conclusion
Management of sleep fragmentation and insomnia in PD should start with cause-focused evaluation and optimization of dopaminergic/night-time symptom control, then add CBT‑I and/or bright light therapy, and, when needed, melatonin, rotigotine, adjusted levodopa regimens or cautious hypnotic use, tailored to comorbidities and daytime sleepiness.

column3: 如何处理帕金森患者并发的“不宁腿综合征”？
column4: How to manage concomitant restless legs syndrome in Parkinson's patients?
column5:
Management of concomitant restless legs syndrome (RLS) in Parkinson’s disease generally follows standard RLS algorithms, with extra caution about dopamine exposure and sleep comorbidities.
1. Core RLS work‑up and non‑drug measures
In PD with suspected RLS, guidelines emphasize:
 Confirming true RLS and not mimics (akathisia, neuropathic pain, leg dystonia).
 Check iron status and replace if ferritin <75–100 µg/L; consider IV iron when ferritin <100 µg/L with severe symptoms or oral failure (Silber et al., 2021; González-Parejo et al., 2024).
 Review and minimize drugs that worsen RLS (some antidepressants, sedating antihistamines, antipsychotics) (Silber et al., 2021; González-Parejo et al., 2024).
 Address co‑occurring sleep disorders, especially OSA, which is common in PD; CPAP can improve RLS symptoms and reduce RLS medication needs in many patients (Tam et al., 2025).
 Behavioral measures: mental alerting activities in the evening, avoiding caffeine/alcohol near bedtime, regular sleep schedule, exercise (Silber et al., 2021; González-Parejo et al., 2024).
 
Selected supplements (adjunctive)
Magnesium oxide, vitamin B6, vitamins C and E, and oral iron each showed improvement in RLS severity and/or sleep in small RCTs, but with high risk of bias; they are best viewed as complements, not replacements, for standard therapy (González-Parejo et al., 2024).
2. Pharmacologic treatment in PD‑RLS
For chronic persistent RLS (symptoms ≥2x/week with distress), updated algorithms now prefer:
 First‑line: α2‑δ ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin): improve sensory symptoms, insomnia, and sleep efficiency, and avoid dopaminergic augmentation risk (Silber et al., 2021; Mogavero et al., 2025). Particularly attractive in PD where patients already receive dopaminergic drugs.
 Second‑line: Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole, rotigotine) when α2‑δ ligands are ineffective or contraindicated, using lowest effective doses and monitoring carefully for augmentation and impulse‑control disorders (Silber et al., 2021; Mogavero et al., 2025).
 Intermittent RLS: on‑demand levodopa, benzodiazepines, or low‑potency opioids (codeine, tramadol) can be used for occasional symptoms (Silber et al., 2021).
 Refractory RLS: recheck iron; use combination therapy (α2‑δ ligand + dopamine agonist, or add benzodiazepine); if still severe, carefully selected opioid monotherapy (e.g., low‑dose methadone, oxycodone) is effective and rarely leads to misuse when patients are appropriately screened (Silber et al., 2021).
 
Summary Table: Key Steps
   Step
 Practical focus in PD‑RLS
 Citations
 
    1. Confirm & stage
 Differentiate from PD motor symptoms; rate severity
 (Silber et al., 2021; González-Parejo et al., 2024)
 
  2. Correct contributors
 Iron deficiency, exacerbating meds, OSA/insomnia
 (Silber et al., 2021; González-Parejo et al., 2024; Tam et al., 2025)
 
  3. First‑line drug
 α2‑δ ligand (gabapentin/pregabalin) for chronic RLS
 (Silber et al., 2021; Mogavero et al., 2025)
 
  4. Second‑line
 Low‑dose dopamine agonist, monitor augmentation
 (Silber et al., 2021; Mogavero et al., 2025)
 
  5. Refractory
 Combination therapy; then cautiously consider opioids
 (Silber et al., 2021)
 
  
Figure 1: Stepwise algorithm for managing RLS in PD
Conclusion
In Parkinson’s patients, concomitant RLS is best managed by systematic evaluation (iron, comorbid sleep disorders, medications), non‑drug and supplement strategies, then α2‑δ ligands as first‑line for chronic symptoms, reserving extra dopaminergic therapy and, in refractory cases, opioids. Careful monitoring is essential because PD patients already have high dopaminergic exposure and complex sleep pathology.

column3: 帕金森病合并膀胱过度活动症（尿频尿急）如何治疗？
column4: What are the treatments for overactive bladder in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Overactive bladder in Parkinson’s is treated with fluid/behavioral measures, then antimuscarinics or β3‑agonists, and in refractory cases neuromodulation or intravesical botulinum toxin A.
Treatment Approach in Parkinson’s Disease
1. Non‑pharmacologic measures (first line)
 Bladder training and pelvic floor exercises are recommended for neurogenic urinary urgency/overactive bladder in neurological disease, including PD (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 Adjust fluid intake (avoid excess fluid in late day), reduce caffeine, alcohol, and carbonated drinks; manage constipation and diuretics (Nambiar et al., 2022; Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 Timed/ prompted voiding and bladder diaries help structure voiding and identify nocturnal polyuria vs small bladder capacity (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 
2. Oral pharmacologic therapy
   Class
 Role in PD overactive bladder
 Citations
 
    Antimuscarinics (solifenacin, trospium, darifenacin)
 Improve urgency and urge incontinence in neurogenic OAB; PD guideline prefers agents with lower CNS penetration to reduce cognitive risk (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025)
 
  β3‑agonist (mirabegron)
 Recommended for neurogenic storage (OAB) symptoms; similar efficacy to antimuscarinics with better safety, useful when antimuscarinics are ineffective/poorly tolerated or contraindicated (eg, cognitive impairment) (Panicker et al., 2025).
 (Panicker et al., 2025)
 
  
Antimuscarinics and β3‑agonists are core first‑line drugs for OAB in neurologic disease and general populations (Nambiar et al., 2022; Panicker et al., 2025; Cameron et al., 2024; Pereca et al., 2025). Long‑term anticholinergic use in older/cognitively vulnerable patients requires caution (Nambiar et al., 2022).
3. Minimally invasive / advanced therapies
 Percutaneous or transcutaneous tibial nerve stimulation improves overactive bladder symptoms in PD and other neurogenic conditions (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 Intravesical botulinum toxin A reduces detrusor overactivity and incontinence when oral therapy fails, but may cause urinary retention; PD guidelines restrict this to patients able to perform intermittent self‑catheterization and without severe motor/cognitive impairment (Panicker et al., 2025; Fanciulli et al., 2025).
 Standard neurogenic bladder guidelines also consider sacral neuromodulation and, rarely, augmentation cystoplasty in severe refractory neurogenic OAB (Ginsberg et al., 2021; Panicker et al., 2025; Ginsberg et al., 2021).
 
4. Nocturia‑specific measures in PD
For predominant nocturia: evening fluid restriction, head‑up tilt sleeping; desmopressin at bedtime can be considered for nocturnal polyuria with close monitoring of blood pressure, electrolytes, and weight (Fanciulli et al., 2025; Panicker et al., 2025).
Conclusion
Management of overactive bladder in Parkinson’s starts with bladder training and lifestyle modification, progresses to antimuscarinics (cognition‑sparing) or mirabegron, and, if needed, tibial nerve stimulation or intravesical botulinum toxin A, with desmopressin for carefully selected nocturia cases.

column4: What is the role of botulinum toxin in managing sialorrhea in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Botulinum toxin is an effective, minimally invasive treatment for troublesome sialorrhea in Parkinson’s disease, used when simple measures or oral drugs are inadequate.
Figure 1: Evidence consensus on BoNT for PD sialorrhea
Role, Efficacy, and Indications
 Sialorrhea in PD is usually due to impaired swallowing rather than excess saliva; botulinum toxin (BoNT) injected into salivary glands reduces saliva production by blocking acetylcholine release at parasympathetic nerve terminals (Ban et al., 2023).
 A systematic review of pharmacologic therapies in PD sialorrhea found that BoNT type A and B significantly reduced drooling burden in randomized and controlled studies, with generally mild adverse effects (Reid et al., 2021).
 Because oral anticholinergics often cause cognitive and systemic side effects in older PD patients, guidelines and reviews position BoNT as a key option when pharmacotherapy is ineffective or poorly tolerated (Ban et al., 2023; Reid et al., 2021; Silva et al., 2024).
 
Injection Technique, Dosing, and Duration
 Injections are typically given into parotid and/or submandibular glands, often under ultrasound guidance to improve accuracy and minimize complications (Ban et al., 2023; Orriëns et al., 2023).
 Reported dose ranges for BoNT‑A are roughly 5–30 U for each submandibular gland and 30–100 U (sometimes higher) per parotid gland, tailored to age, weight, and severity (Ban et al., 2023).
 Onset occurs within about 1 week and effects last ~3–5 months, with some PD trials showing benefit up to 13–20 weeks (Ban et al., 2023). Repeat injections are scheduled based on symptom recurrence and tolerability (Ban et al., 2023; Reid et al., 2021).
 
Benefits and Risks
   Aspect
 Summary in PD/neurologic drooling
 Citations
 
    Symptom control
 Clear reduction in drooling severity and frequency; improved QoL for patients/caregivers
 (Reid et al., 2021; Silva et al., 2024; Ban et al., 2023)
 
  Adverse effects
 Usually mild; include dry mouth, thickened saliva; dose‑related dysphagia and rare sialadenitis at higher doses
 (Ban et al., 2023; Reid et al., 2021)
 
  Comparative role
 Less systemic burden than anticholinergics; less invasive than surgery or radiotherapy
 (Ban et al., 2023; Silva et al., 2024; Reid et al., 2021)
 
  
Figure 2: Benefits and risks of BoNT for drooling
Conclusion
For Parkinson’s-related sialorrhea, botulinum toxin injections into the salivary glands are a well‑supported, repeatable, and relatively safe option when conservative and oral treatments are inadequate, with benefits on drooling and quality of life but requiring specialist administration and monitoring for dysphagia and xerostomia.

column4: What are the ideal candidate criteria for Deep Brain Stimulation in PD?
column5:
Deep brain stimulation in Parkinson’s disease is best for levodopa‑responsive patients with disabling motor fluctuations or tremor, preserved cognition, and manageable psychiatric/medical comorbidities.
Core Candidacy Criteria
Evidence‑based guidelines and systematic reviews describe a fairly consistent candidate profile for DBS in PD (Reese et al., 2025; Sisodia et al., 2024; De Bie et al., 2025; Ahmed et al., 2025):
1. Disease and Symptom Profile
 Idiopathic PD with clear diagnosis; atypical parkinsonism is a red flag (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025).
 Disabling motor fluctuations and/or troublesome dyskinesias despite optimized oral dopaminergic therapy, or severe medication‑resistant tremor (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025).
 Good levodopa responsiveness (typically ≥30% improvement in UPDRS III OFF→ON) predicts better motor benefit (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025).
 
2. Cognitive and Psychiatric Status
 No dementia; only mild cognitive impairment at most. Pre‑op cognition does not abolish motor benefit but is crucial for safety and quality of life (Sisodia et al., 2024).
 Absence of uncontrolled major psychiatric illness (psychosis, severe depression, active suicidality); psychiatric comorbidity and early post‑DBS psychiatric AEs are common and must be anticipated (Ahmed et al., 2025).
 Realistic treatment expectations and good psychosocial support are important predictors of satisfaction and adjustment (Mameli et al., 2023).
 
3. Age, Disability, and Overall Health
 Best evidence in younger/biologically fit patients; advanced age alone is not an absolute contraindication, but higher risk of cognitive and psychiatric complications requires caution (Reese et al., 2025; Ahmed et al., 2025).
 No prohibitive surgical or anesthetic risk (e.g., uncontrolled cardiovascular disease, coagulopathy) (Reese et al., 2025).
 
4. Practical Requirements
 Ability and willingness to attend long‑term follow‑up and programming, and to participate in adjustments of dopaminergic medication after surgery (Reese et al., 2025; De Bie et al., 2025).
 
Conclusion
Ideal DBS candidates with PD are those with idiopathic, levodopa‑responsive disease; severe motor complications despite optimal drugs; preserved cognition and controlled mood/psychosis; acceptable surgical risk; and realistic expectations, evaluated by a multidisciplinary team.

column3: STN靶点与GPi靶点的DBS手术在预后上有什么区别？
column4: What are the differences in outcomes between STN and GPi DBS for Parkinson's?
column5:
STN and GPi DBS provide similar overall motor benefit in Parkinson’s disease, but differ in medication reduction and side‑effect profile.
Motor Outcomes and Tremor
 Large RCTs and guideline reviews show comparable improvement in UPDRS III motor scores with bilateral STN vs GPi DBS at 1–3 years (De Bie et al., 2025; Reese et al., 2025).
 One RCT found greater off‑medication motor improvement with STN than GPi (≈20 vs 11 UPDRS III points) (De Bie et al., 2025; Reese et al., 2025).
 Meta‑analysis of tremor outcomes showed no significant long‑term difference in tremor suppression between STN and GPi, both with moderate effect sizes (Reese et al., 2025).
 
Key Clinical Differences
   Domain
 STN DBS
 GPi DBS
 Citations
 
    Motor benefit
 Similar to GPi overall; sometimes slightly greater OFF‑med motor gain
 Similar overall; may be slightly less OFF benefit in some trials
 (De Bie et al., 2025; Reese et al., 2025)
 
  Levodopa dose
 Substantial reduction typical
 Usually smaller reduction
 (Kokkonen et al., 2022; De Bie et al., 2025; Reese et al., 2025)
 
  Dyskinesias
 Improved, largely via med reduction
 Direct anti‑dyskinetic effect; strong choice for dyskinesia‑dominant PD
 (De Bie et al., 2025; Reese et al., 2025)
 
  Cognition/psychiatry
 Greater concern for verbal fluency decline and cognitive worsening over time in some cohorts
 Often favored when cognition or mood is fragile
 (De Bie et al., 2025; Baudouin et al., 2022; Ahmed et al., 2024; Reese et al., 2025)
 
  Speech/voice
 More studies report deterioration in speech/voice
 Pallidal stimulation more often linked to better or stable voice
 (Baudouin et al., 2022; Tabari et al., 2024)
 
  
Figure 1: Contrasting clinical effects of STN vs GPi
Non‑motor & Safety Considerations
 Cognitive decline (e.g., dementia rating scale) progressed faster in STN than GPi in a long‑term RCT, though baseline cognition differed slightly (De Bie et al., 2025).
 STN DBS is more often associated with speech problems and gait/balance issues, while GPi DBS may better preserve voice and speech in some series (Ahmed et al., 2024; Baudouin et al., 2022; Tabari et al., 2024).
 Molecular imaging and fMRI suggest STN produces broader network changes and allows more dopaminergic reduction, which may underlie both its benefits and side‑effects (Kokkonen et al., 2022; Loh et al., 2022).
 
Conclusion
STN and GPi DBS are equally effective for core motor control, but STN is preferred when medication reduction is a priority, whereas GPi is often chosen for prominent dyskinesias, speech/cognitive vulnerability, or frailer patients. Choice is best individualized by a multidisciplinary DBS team.

column3: 左旋多巴-卡比多巴肠胶（LCIG）灌注疗法的适应症是什么？
column4: What are the indications for Levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel (LCIG) therapy?
column5:
Levodopa–carbidopa intestinal gel is indicated for advanced, levodopa‑responsive Parkinson’s disease with disabling motor fluctuations when optimized oral therapy is no longer adequate.
Core Indications
1. Disease stage and motor complications
 Advanced PD with severe treatment‑related motor fluctuations and/or troublesome dyskinesias despite optimized oral levodopa and adjuncts (COMT/MAO‑B inhibitors, etc.) (Tsunemi et al., 2021; De Bie et al., 2025).
 Clear levodopa responsiveness; LCIG is intended as a device‑aided way to deliver levodopa continuously, not as a diagnostic trial (Tsunemi et al., 2021; De Bie et al., 2025).
 
2. Symptom profile favoring LCIG over other options
 Patients needing reduction in daily OFF time and increase in ON time without troublesome dyskinesia; RCT data show ≈2 h less OFF and ≈2 h more good ON vs immediate‑release oral levodopa (De Bie et al., 2025).
 Advanced PD with predominant non‑motor symptoms (sleep problems, impulse‑control disorders, pain, fatigue) or high caregiver burden, where LCIG may improve non‑motor symptoms, activities of daily living, quality of life, and reduce caregiver strain (Tsunemi et al., 2021).
 Patients with motor fluctuations and ICDs who are candidates for continuous non‑oral dopaminergic therapy; LCIG can markedly improve ICD scores when used for a motor indication (Witt et al., 2024).
 
Practical/Economic Guideline Context
 Included as a high‑priority device‑aided therapy in national and international guidance for advanced PD alongside DBS and apomorphine infusion (Tsunemi et al., 2021; Norlin et al., 2021).
 Considered “likely efficacious” for motor fluctuations in patients already on attempted optimal oral levodopa in recent evidence‑based reviews (De Bie et al., 2025).
 
Conclusion
In practice, LCIG is chosen for advanced, levodopa‑responsive PD with severe motor fluctuations and/or dyskinesia, often with important non‑motor symptoms or caregiver burden, when oral regimens fail and a continuous, adjustable levodopa infusion is acceptable and feasible.

column4: When should subcutaneous apomorphine infusion be considered?
column5:
Continuous subcutaneous apomorphine infusion is considered for advanced, levodopa‑responsive Parkinson’s disease with disabling motor fluctuations despite optimized oral therapy.
Core Indication
 Evidence‑based MDS review rates continuous subcutaneous apomorphine infusion (CSAI) as “likely efficacious” for treating motor fluctuations in patients already on (attempted) optimal oral levodopa (De Bie et al., 2025).
 In the pivotal RCT (~16 h/day infusion), CSAI reduced daily OFF time by ≈1.9 h and increased ON time without troublesome dyskinesia by ≈2 h vs placebo, in patients with significant motor fluctuations on levodopa (De Bie et al., 2025).
 
When to Consider CSAI
   Clinical situation
 Role for CSAI
 Citations
 
    Advanced PD with marked OFF periods despite optimized oral levodopa and add‑ons
 Appropriate device‑aided option to reduce OFF and improve ON without troublesome dyskinesia
 (De Bie et al., 2025; Longo & Papagno, 2025)
 
  Patient is levodopa‑responsive but ineligible or unwilling for DBS (age, comorbidity, preference)
 CSAI or LCIG are main alternatives
 (Longo & Papagno, 2025; Norlin et al., 2021)
 
  Need for less invasive, adjustable continuous dopaminergic therapy
 CSAI favored over intestinal surgery‑based LCIG
 (Longo & Papagno, 2025; Norlin et al., 2021)
 
  Concern about cognitive vulnerability
 Systematic review suggests APO may have a more favorable cognitive profile than LCIG, though data are non‑randomized and biased (Longo & Papagno, 2025)
 
 
  
Figure 1: Main clinical scenarios supporting CSAI use
What It Is Not Used For
 RCT‑level evidence is insufficient to support intermittent apomorphine (pens, sublingual film) as chronic therapy for OFF or QoL improvement; these are rescue treatments, not continuous therapy substitutes (De Bie et al., 2025).
 
Conclusion
In practice, continuous subcutaneous apomorphine infusion is considered for advanced, levodopa‑responsive PD with severe motor fluctuations unmet by optimized oral regimens, particularly when a non‑surgical, adjustable infusion is preferred or DBS is not suitable, with some evidence suggesting relatively preserved cognition.

column3: 高强度有氧运动能延缓帕金森病的进展吗？
column4: Does high-intensity aerobic exercise slow Parkinson's disease progression?
column5:
While high‑intensity aerobic exercise shows promising disease‑modifying potential, evidence that it definitively slows progression of Parkinson’s disease is still emerging and not conclusive.
Figure 1: Overall research leaning but not definitive
What Current Research Shows
Aerobic & high‑intensity exercise benefits
 Umbrella and systematic reviews show aerobic exercise (including vigorous and interval protocols) improves motor function, gait, balance, mobility, and cardiorespiratory fitness in mild–moderate PD (Padilha et al., 2023; De Oliveira et al., 2021).
 High‑quality studies within an umbrella review found aerobic (often moderate–vigorous) training improves walking, motor function, and endurance and may attenuate motor symptoms even off medication (Padilha et al., 2023).
 In early/de novo PD, high‑intensity treadmill exercise (≈80–85% max heart rate) is feasible and safe, improves VO₂max, and prevents decline in fitness compared with usual care, but trials to test clinical disease‑modifying effects are still pending (Gilby et al., 2025).
 
Evidence for slowing progression
   Evidence type
 Key points
 Citations
 
    Human clinical trials
 Some longitudinal and early‑PD trials were designed to test whether intensive/high‑intensity programs slow progression, but so far mainly show functional and fitness gains; clear slowing of clinical progression is not yet firmly proven (Gilby et al., 2025).
 (Gilby et al., 2025)
 
  Systematic/umbrella reviews
 Frequently state that exercise, especially aerobic, may delay or slow progression, but emphasize that optimal dose (intensity/frequency) and causal proof of neuroprotection remain uncertain (Padilha et al., 2023; Gilby et al., 2025; Khan et al., 2024).
 (Padilha et al., 2023; Gilby et al., 2025; Khan et al., 2024)
 
  Animal models
 Treadmill exercise in PD models improves dopaminergic markers, neuroplasticity, and reduces neuroinflammation, supporting a biological basis for neuroprotection, but translation to humans is indirect (Ferreira et al., 2021).
 (Ferreira et al., 2021)
 
  
Figure 2: Summary of evidence for progression slowing
Conclusion
High‑intensity aerobic exercise clearly improves motor performance, gait, mobility, and fitness in Parkinson’s disease and is biologically plausible as neuroprotective, but definitive proof that it slows disease progression in humans is still lacking. Regular, appropriately supervised aerobic exercise (often at moderate–vigorous intensity) is strongly supported as part of PD management while research on true disease modification continues.

column3: 磁共振引导聚焦超声（MRgFUS）在帕金森病中的应用现状？
column4: What is the role of MRI-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
MRgFUS is an emerging ablative option mainly for unilateral tremor control in Parkinson’s disease, used when deep brain stimulation is unsuitable.
Clinical Role in Parkinson’s Disease
Main current indication: tremor control
 German Neurology guidelines on invasive PD therapies state that MR‑guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) thalamotomy in the ventral intermediate nucleus (VIM) can be considered for unilateral, tremor‑dominant PD when DBS is not suitable (Reese et al., 2025).
 MRgFUS creates a permanent lesion (ablative therapy) without craniotomy or implanted hardware, offering a less invasive alternative to radiofrequency thalamotomy or DBS leads (Reese et al., 2025).
 
Position among invasive therapies
   Situation
 Role of MRgFUS
 Citations
 
    Advanced PD with disabling, medication‑refractory unilateral tremor, relatively preserved other motor features
 Option for unilateral VIM thalamotomy; tremor reduction can be substantial but only on treated side
 (Reese et al., 2025; Pellow et al., 2024)
 
  Patients who are poor candidates for DBS (age, comorbidities, anticoagulation, or refusal of implants)
 Non‑incisional, no hardware, outpatient; therefore attractive alternative ablative approach
 (Reese et al., 2025; Pellow et al., 2024)
 
  Prominent bradykinesia, rigidity, or motor fluctuations
 MRgFUS VIM thalamotomy does not address broader motor fluctuations; DBS or pump therapies remain preferred
 (Reese et al., 2025)
 
  Bilateral tremor
 Bilateral MRgFUS is experimental; risk of dysarthria/gait and cognitive effects limits routine bilateral use
 (Reese et al., 2025; Pellow et al., 2024)
 
  
Figure 1: Comparative role of MRgFUS within PD therapies
Experimental and emerging uses
 Preliminary work is exploring MRgFUS lesions in subthalamic nucleus (STN) for broader PD motor symptoms, but guideline authors note it is unclear whether STN MRgFUS matches DBS in efficacy or durability, so it should be used only in carefully selected/research contexts (Reese et al., 2025).
 Functional MRI–based connectomic analyses show MRgFUS VIM thalamotomy modulates tremor‑related networks in PD, paralleling essential tremor findings, supporting its mechanistic rationale but not yet expanding indications (Pellow et al., 2024).
 
Conclusion
MRgFUS in PD is currently best viewed as a non‑invasive, unilateral ablative option for drug‑ and DBS‑refractory tremor, not a replacement for DBS or pump therapies for global motor fluctuations. Its role beyond tremor (e.g., STN targets, bilateral use) remains investigational and should be confined to expert centers and clinical studies.

column3: 哪些具体的物理治疗（PT）技术可以改善帕金森的步态？
column4: What specific physical therapy techniques improve gait in Parkinson's?
column5:
Several specific PT techniques improve gait in Parkinson’s, especially task‑specific gait training with external cues, aerobic/strength work, and technology‑assisted or mirror‑neuron–based training.
Key Physical Therapy Techniques for Gait
1. Task‑specific gait training & treadmills
 Treadmill gait training (including body‑weight–supported or robotic Lokomat) improves 6‑minute walk distance and gait performance; robotic systems are not superior to conventional treadmill at similar intensities (Gilby et al., 2025).
 Aerobic treadmill programs improve walking speed and stride length and overall mobility in mild–moderate PD (De Oliveira et al., 2021).
 
2. External cueing during walking
 Systematic review of 10 RCTs: walking training + auditory cueing (metronome/music) improves walking speed more than walking training alone (mean +0.09 m/s) (Nascimento et al., 2024).
 Visual cueing added to walking has less consistent benefit for speed/stride length (Nascimento et al., 2024).
 
3. Strength and resistance training
 Resistance exercise yields very‑low‑quality but positive evidence for improving gait activity measures in mild–moderate PD (De Oliveira et al., 2021).
 Combined strength + functional task training supports better mobility and fall prevention (Padilha et al., 2023).
 
4. Mirror‑neuron–based strategies (Action Observation & Motor Imagery)
 Systematic review: AO/MI‑based therapies improve gait, balance, and disease severity in PD, using repeated practice of observed/imagined gait tasks (Lahuerta-Martín et al., 2022).
 A scoping review confirms several AO/MI studies show gait and freezing improvements, though not all trials are positive (Tedeschi et al., 2024).
 
5. Technology‑assisted and virtual reality approaches
 VR treadmill/exergame systems significantly improve gait and balance compared with traditional PT in older adults with PD (Bevilacqua et al., 2023; Rodríguez-Mansilla et al., 2023).
 Gamified telerehabilitation and CMDT (walking plus cognitive tasks with motion‑tracking/VR) improve gait performance and dual‑task walking, with good safety and adherence (Norouzi-Ghazbi et al., 2025; Tuena et al., 2022).
 
Summary of Main Techniques
   Technique type
 Primary gait benefits
 Citations
 
    Treadmill / BW‑supported gait
 Speed, endurance, stride length
 (Gilby et al., 2025; De Oliveira et al., 2021)
 
  Auditory cueing during walking
 Increased walking speed
 (Nascimento et al., 2024)
 
  Resistance/strength training
 Gait activity measures
 (De Oliveira et al., 2021; Padilha et al., 2023)
 
  AO/MI (mirror‑neuron)
 Gait, balance, FOG (variable)
 (Lahuerta-Martín et al., 2022; Tedeschi et al., 2024)
 
  VR / exergames / CMDT
 Gait, balance, dual‑tasking
 (Bevilacqua et al., 2023; Rodríguez-Mansilla et al., 2023; Norouzi-Ghazbi et al., 2025; Tuena et al., 2022)
 
  
Figure 1: Major PT gait techniques and benefits in PD
Conclusion
Best‑supported PT for gait in Parkinson’s combines task‑specific gait practice (often on a treadmill), auditory cueing, strengthening, and where available, AO/MI or technology‑assisted VR/dual‑task training, all individualized to disease stage and fall risk.

column3: LSVT LOUD言语治疗对帕金森声音嘶哑/发音不清有多大效果？
column4: How effective is LSVT LOUD for speech disorders in Parkinson's disease?
column5:
LSVT LOUD provides clinically meaningful, often large improvements in vocal loudness and intelligibility in Parkinson’s, with effects that can persist for months to years.
Figure 1: Overall evidence that LSVT LOUD is effective
Magnitude and Type of Benefit
 Randomized and controlled studies show increased sound pressure level (loudness) and improved speech intelligibility after the standard 4‑week LSVT LOUD protocol, with gains often reaching normal or near‑normal loudness in many participants (Ngo et al., 2022).
 Acoustic analyses demonstrate improvements in voice quality, pitch variability, and articulatory precision, addressing hypophonia and monotonous speech typical of PD (Ngo et al., 2022).
 Computerized speech analyses detect measurable changes in prosody and articulation consistent with clinical gains after intensive speech interventions like LSVT LOUD (Ngo et al., 2022).
 
Durability and Generalization
 Follow‑up data (6–24 months) in earlier LSVT LOUD trials report maintenance of louder voice and better intelligibility for many patients, especially those who continue some home practice, indicating reasonably durable effects for a behavioral therapy (Ngo et al., 2022).
 Improvements generalize beyond single words to sentences and conversational speech, and some studies report better communication ratings by patients and caregivers (Ngo et al., 2022).
 
Limitations and Practical Considerations
 Effect sizes are generally large, but response is variable, with less benefit in advanced disease or significant cognitive impairment (Ngo et al., 2022).
 Treatment is intensive (4 sessions/week for 4 weeks, daily homework), which may limit accessibility; telepractice and technology‑assisted delivery are being explored but remain less studied (Ngo et al., 2022).
 Most evidence focuses on hypophonia/voice; benefits for other speech dimensions (e.g., severe dysarthria) are more limited.
 
Conclusion
Across controlled trials and acoustic studies, LSVT LOUD is a well‑supported, first‑line behavioral treatment for PD‑related hypophonia and related speech deficits, providing substantial and sometimes long‑lasting improvement in loudness and intelligibility when patients can tolerate the intensive program.

column3: 目前有证实具有神经保护作用的帕金森膳食补充剂吗？
column4: Are there any neuroprotective dietary supplements for Parkinson's disease?
column5:
Evidence for neuroprotective supplements in Parkinson’s is mostly preclinical, with only limited, early human data.
Figure 1: Evidence strength for neuroprotective supplements in PD
What Looks Promising (But Not Proven in Humans)
1. Dietary flavonoids and polyphenols
 Animal PD models: many natural antioxidants (curcumin, resveratrol, kaempferol, quercetin, phenolic acids) reduce oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, α‑synuclein aggregation, and dopaminergic neuron loss, improving motor behavior (Jin et al., 2023; Costas & Faro, 2021; Liang et al., 2024; Perdigão et al., 2023).
 Human data: a systematic review found that higher intake of flavonoids/anthocyanins and foods like apples, berries, and moderate red wine was associated with lower PD risk and mortality; small RCTs with cocoa, licorice, or curcumin improved motor scores but did not show slowed progression (Adrián et al., 2023).
 Critical analysis shows most experimental doses are far above what is achievable with normal diet, limiting direct translation (Perdigão et al., 2023).
 
2. Probiotics and gut–brain targeting
 Preclinical PD models: probiotics reduce peripheral/central inflammation, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic neuron loss, and improve motor and cognitive function (Leta et al., 2021).
 Small clinical trials: improved constipation, some reduction in systemic inflammation and overall symptom scores, but no clear disease‑modifying effect yet (Leta et al., 2021).
 
3. Vitamins and nitrate
 Antioxidant vitamins (A, C, E, B‑vitamins) and other dietary antioxidants may lower PD risk and support dopaminergic neuron survival, mainly from observational and animal data (Costas & Faro, 2021; Perdigão et al., 2023; Savran et al., 2025).
 Vitamin B6/B12/folate are recommended mainly to counter levodopa‑induced hyperhomocysteinemia and neuropathy, not as proven neuroprotective agents (Keun et al., 2021; , 2021).
 Dietary nitrate (e.g., beetroot, leafy greens) may improve cerebral blood flow, mitochondrial efficiency, and reduce oxidative damage, but PD neuroprotection remains hypothetical and needs trials (Tripodi et al., 2025).
 
4. Other phytochemicals
Compounds like kaempferol, ginsenosides, puerarin and others show robust neuroprotection in cell/animal PD models via antioxidant, anti‑inflammatory and mitochondrial/mitophagy pathways (Jin et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2025; Liang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023), but have no convincing PD clinical trial evidence.
Key Takeaway Table
   Category
 Human PD evidence for neuroprotection
 Citations
 
    Flavonoid‑rich diet/polyphenols
 Suggest reduced risk; symptom benefit, no proven slowing
 (Costas & Faro, 2021; Adrián et al., 2023; Perdigão et al., 2023; Bianchi et al., 2022)
 
  Probiotics
 Symptom and inflammatory improvements; neuroprotection unproven
 (Leta et al., 2021; Kurhaluk et al., 2025)
 
  Vitamins (A,C,E,Bs,D)
 Observational risk links, metabolic support; no clear disease‑modifying trial
 (Costas & Faro, 2021; Keun et al., 2021; , 2021; Savran et al., 2025)
 
  Specific plant compounds (curcumin, ginsenosides, kaempferol, puerarin, quercetin)
 Strong animal data only
 (Jin et al., 2023; Lehoczki et al., 2025; Jiang et al., 2025; Liang et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023; Islam et al., 2025)
 
  
Figure 2: Overview of supplement classes and human evidence
Conclusion
Many supplements show neuroprotective effects in laboratory and animal PD models, and diets rich in flavonoids/antioxidants may lower PD risk, but no supplement has definitive clinical evidence of slowing disease progression in people with Parkinson’s. Any use should be adjunctive, checked for drug interactions (especially with levodopa), and ideally supervised by a clinician.

column3: 多学科团队（MDT）护理对帕金森患者的临床结局有何影响？
column4: What is the impact of multidisciplinary team care on Parkinson's disease outcomes?
column5:
Multidisciplinary team (MDT) care generally improves quality of life and several clinical outcomes in Parkinson’s disease, especially when nurse specialists coordinate care.
Figure 1: Consensus on MDT care benefit in Parkinson’s
What MDT Care Looks Like in Parkinson’s
MDT care typically involves neurologists, Parkinson’s disease nurse specialists (PDNS), physiotherapists, occupational and speech therapists, psychologists, social workers and palliative care working in a coordinated way (Fujita et al., 2024). Nurses often act as liaisons and coordinators, performing overall assessment, treatment management, safety/fall assessment, non‑motor symptom care, caregiver support, staff education, and linkage to social resources (Fujita et al., 2024).
Impact on Patient Outcomes
Studies of home‑based and community MDT programs with PD nurses report improvements in:
 Emotional well‑being, stigma, communication and physical discomfort when PDNS collaborate closely with community neurologists and movement‑disorder specialists (Fujita et al., 2024).
 Medication adherence, safety, dysphagia, mental health and caregiver support, which are handled more systematically through coordinated nursing‑led MDT visits and telenursing (Fujita et al., 2024).
 Quality of palliative care at advanced stages, where lack of coordination between physicians and specialist nurses is linked to poorer end‑of‑life care; integrated palliative–neurology MDT models are recommended to improve quality of life near end of life (Akbar et al., 2021; Fujita et al., 2024).
 
Technology‑assisted rehabilitation reviews emphasize that optimal use of novel interventions for gait and balance in older adults with PD requires a multidisciplinary, multicomponent, personalized approach, integrating physical and psychological services (Bevilacqua et al., 2023).

Conclusion
Across home, rehabilitation, and palliative contexts, MDT care—particularly when coordinated by PD nurse specialists—improves quality of life, symptom control, safety, and communication in Parkinson’s disease, although high‑quality comparative trials are still limited.
